critic_raw: The multimodal neural network for post-operative complication prediction has two methodological concerns. First, the primary evaluation metric is sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity. This metric is computed by selecting a decision threshold on the validation set that achieves 85% specificity, then applying that threshold to the test set. However, the test set's class distribution may differ from the validation set's class distribution due to the temporal split — later months may have higher or lower complication rates due to protocol changes or seasonal effects. When the validation-derived threshold is applied to the test set, the achieved specificity on the test set may deviate substantially from 85%, making the primary metric non-comparable between models. A threshold-free metric like AUROC would avoid this problem. Second, the surgeon identifier is included as a fixed effect feature in the model. In a small hospital system over 5 years, a surgeon who retires or joins mid-study may appear only in training or only in test. Neither situation is addressed.
all_issues_raised: Sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity requires threshold transfer from validation to test; if complication rate shifts between these temporal periods, the threshold-transferred specificity on test may deviate substantially from 85%, invalidating the primary metric comparison.
Surgeon identifier as a fixed effect feature creates out-of-vocabulary issues at test time when surgeons join or retire mid-study; handling of unseen surgeon identifiers is not addressed.

round_transcripts.critic: The multimodal neural network for post-operative complication prediction has two methodological concerns. First, the primary evaluation metric is sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity. This metric is computed by selecting a decision threshold on the validation set that achieves 85% specificity, then applying that threshold to the test set. However, the test set's class distribution may differ from the validation set's class distribution due to the temporal split — later months may have higher or lower complication rates due to protocol changes or seasonal effects. When the validation-derived threshold is applied to the test set, the achieved specificity on the test set may deviate substantially from 85%, making the primary metric non-comparable between models. A threshold-free metric like AUROC would avoid this problem. Second, the surgeon identifier is included as a fixed effect feature in the model. In a small hospital system over 5 years, a surgeon who retires or joins mid-study may appear only in training or only in test. Neither situation is addressed.
round_transcripts.defender: On threshold transfer: the design reports AUROC as a secondary metric precisely to provide a threshold-free complement to the primary metric. If the achieved test specificity deviates from 85%, the appropriate response is to report the actual achieved specificity alongside sensitivity, making clear what operating point was tested. The design already includes AUROC as a backstop. On surgeon identifiers: the design includes stratification by surgeon in the temporal split, ensuring each surgeon appears proportionally across all three sets. Surgeons who join or retire during the 5-year period are an edge case — the design should specify that new surgeons in the test period are assigned to an 'unknown surgeon' embedding initialized from the training distribution mean. This is standard handling for unseen categorical levels and is straightforward to implement.

round_transcripts.critic: On threshold transfer: the defense correctly notes that AUROC is a secondary metric. However, making sensitivity at 85% specificity the primary metric while knowing that achieved test specificity may deviate from 85% means the primary metric is unreliable as a performance summary. If two models are compared on this metric but achieve different actual specificities on the test set (say 82% vs. 88%), their sensitivity values are not comparable. The design should either use AUROC as primary or specify that sensitivity is reported at the actual achieved specificity on the test set, not the targeted 85%. On surgeon identifiers: the defense proposes using a mean-initialized embedding for unseen surgeons. This is reasonable but is not specified in the design. Without this specification, the handling of new surgeons is undefined.
round_transcripts.defender: On threshold: the defense concedes that sensitivity at 85% specificity is a problematic primary metric if the target specificity is set on validation and evaluated on test. The design should be revised to use AUROC as the primary metric and report sensitivity at the achieved test specificity as a secondary operational metric. On surgeon identifiers: the mean embedding fallback should be explicitly specified in the preprocessing section. These are both addressable design clarifications.

all_issues_adjudicated: Primary metric (sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity) is problematic when threshold is transferred from validation to test under temporal distribution shift; design should specify AUROC as primary or constrain the specificity targeting.
